source.title: Catastrophic Rupture of Heat Exchanger (Seven Fatalities) - Tesoro Anacortes Refinery

event.top_event: Catastrophic rupture of heat exchanger E-6600E
event.incident_type: Loss of Containment - Explosion and Fire
actions_taken: Heat exchanger rupture released hydrogen and naphtha at >500°F
Autoignition occurred causing explosion and intense fire lasting >3 hours
CSB computer reconstruction of process conditions conducted
Tesoro replacement heat exchangers installed post-incident
event.summary: On April 2, 2010, the Tesoro Anacortes Refinery experienced a catastrophic rupture of heat exchanger E-6600E in the Naphtha Hydrotreater (NHT) unit. The carbon steel heat exchanger failed due to High Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA), releasing highly flammable hydrogen and naphtha at temperatures exceeding 500°F. The release ignited, causing an explosion and fire that burned for over three hours. Seven Tesoro employees (one shift supervisor and six operators) working in the immediate vicinity were fatally injured. This was the largest fatal incident at a US petroleum refinery since the BP Texas City accident in March 2005.
recommendations: EPA should enforce inherently safer design principles in petroleum refineries
Washington State Legislature should strengthen PSM regulations and regulatory oversight
Washington State DOSH should enhance inspection programs and competency
API should revise RP 941 to include minimum requirements and accurate HTHA guidance
API should revise RP 581 to require verification of actual operating conditions
Tesoro should implement inherently safer design using high chromium steels
Tesoro should improve Process Hazard Analysis procedures and effectiveness
Tesoro should enhance control of nonroutine hazardous work
United Steelworkers should participate in process safety oversight

hazards.name: High Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA) in carbon steel equipment
Hydrogen and hydrocarbon release at elevated temperature and pressure
Nonroutine hazardous startup operations with inadequate controls

threats.name: Carbon steel heat exchanger exposed to hydrogen at elevated temperature and pressure above safe operating limits
HTHA damage accumulation in non-post-weld heat-treated welds and high-stress areas
Actual operating conditions exceed design parameters and Nelson curve safe operating region
Inadequate Process Hazard Analysis failing to identify HTHA as credible failure mechanism
Reliance on design operating conditions rather than actual measured operating conditions
Excessive number of workers present during hazardous nonroutine startup operations
Frequent heat exchanger flange leaks during startup creating hazardous conditions

consequences.name: Catastrophic rupture of heat exchanger E-6600E
Release of hydrogen and naphtha at >500°F
Severe thermal and blast injuries to workers in immediate vicinity

controls.name: Material selection - Use of carbon steel for heat exchanger construction
controls.barrier_role: Primary barrier to prevent HTHA damage
controls.lod_basis: Inherently safer design principle - material selection is highest on hierarchy of controls

supporting_text: The rupture of the E heat exchanger was the result of the carbon steel heat exchanger being severely weakened by a damage mechanism known as HTHA.
The B and E heat exchangers were not constructed from these inherently safer materials.
API has identified high chromium steels that are significantly more resistant to HTHA than carbon steel.

controls.name: Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) of heat exchanger welds
controls.barrier_role: Reduce stress concentration in welds susceptible to HTHA
controls.lod_basis: Engineering control to reduce high-stress areas where HTHA accumulates

supporting_text: The welds of the B and E carbon steel heat exchangers were not post-weld heat-treated.
HTHA can accumulate in high-stress areas in carbon steel, such as non-post-weld heat-treated welds.
The high stress areas near the welds of these heat exchangers were found to contain HTHA damage.

controls.name: Nelson curve operating limits for HTHA prevention
controls.barrier_role: Establish safe operating envelope to prevent HTHA
controls.lod_basis: Industry standard guidance for operating conditions to prevent HTHA damage

supporting_text: The CSB performed computer reconstruction of the process conditions within the NHT heat exchangers. The results show that the portion of the carbon steel E heat exchanger that ruptured was estimated to have operated below the applicable Nelson curve.
The carbon steel Nelson curve methodology is inaccurate, cannot be depended on to prevent HTHA equipment failures, and cannot be reliably used to predict the occurrence of HTHA equipment damage.
The CSB has learned of at least eight recent refinery incidents where HTHA reportedly occurred below the carbon steel Nelson curve.

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) to identify HTHA as credible failure mechanism
controls.barrier_role: Identify and evaluate HTHA hazards and recommend safeguards
controls.lod_basis: Required by PSM regulations to identify process hazards and control measures

supporting_text: The 1996 Shell Oil NHT unit PHA simply cited ineffective, non-specific, judgment-based, qualitative safeguards to prevent equipment failure from HTHA.
The 2001 and 2006 Tesoro PHA revalidations did not address or modify the analysis performed in the 1996 Shell Oil PHA.
The Tesoro 2010 NHT unit PHA failed to identify HTHA as a hazard for the shell of the B and E heat exchangers.

For the 15 years before the April 2010 incident, assumptions used by PHA teams at the Anacortes refinery contributed to ineffective safeguards, ineffective hazard identification, and ineffective control of hazards.

controls.name: Damage Mechanism Hazard Review (DMHR) to identify HTHA susceptibility
controls.barrier_role: Identify HTHA as credible damage mechanism for equipment
controls.lod_basis: Technical review to identify damage mechanisms and recommend mitigation

supporting_text: Shell Oil and Tesoro periodically performed damage mechanism hazard reviews (DMHRs), called corrosion reviews. However, these reviews did not identify HTHA as a credible failure mechanism for the B and E heat exchangers.
These reviews were weakened by primarily relying on design operating parameters for these heat exchangers rather than data from actual process operating conditions.
Tesoro did not monitor actual operating conditions of the B and E heat exchangers within the NHT heat exchanger banks, even though it would have been technically feasible to do so.

controls.name: Monitoring and verification of actual operating conditions
controls.barrier_role: Verify that actual operating conditions do not exceed safe limits
controls.lod_basis: Technical control to ensure equipment operates within safe parameters

supporting_text: Tesoro did not monitor actual operating conditions of the B and E heat exchangers within the NHT heat exchanger banks, even though it would have been technically feasible to do so.

Rather, corrosion experts hired by Tesoro primarily relied on design operating conditions that when evaluated using the Nelson curve indicated lower susceptibility to HTHA damage than the operating conditions estimated by CSB models.
If Tesoro had measured or otherwise technically evaluated the actual operating conditions of these heat exchangers, existing company procedures required HTHA inspection.

controls.name: HTHA inspection using nondestructive examination (NDE) techniques
controls.barrier_role: Detect HTHA damage before catastrophic failure
controls.lod_basis: Detection control to identify damage before failure

supporting_text: It is very difficult to inspect for HTHA because the damage might not be detected; it can be microscopic and may be present only in small localized areas of equipment.
Equipment must already be damaged by HTHA for equipment inspection to identify HTHA.
Successful identification of HTHA is highly dependent on the specific techniques employed and the skill of the inspector, and there are few inspectors who have this expertise.
Inspection is therefore not sufficiently reliable to ensure mechanical integrity and prevent HTHA equipment damage.

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) procedure for process modifications
controls.barrier_role: Evaluate impact of changes on HTHA susceptibility
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control to assess hazard implications of process changes

supporting_text: Shell Oil completed a PHA in 1995 related to process modifications that could increase the hydrogen partial pressure in the NHT heat exchangers.
However, when managing this change no consideration, evaluation, or recommendations were made to address the potential for HTHA damage to the NHT heat exchangers.
MOCs did not effectively control hazardous conditions.

controls.name: Control of nonroutine hazardous startup operations
controls.barrier_role: Limit worker exposure during hazardous startup activities
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control to minimize personnel exposure to hazards

supporting_text: Tesoro procedures did not prohibit or effectively limit the use of additional personnel during the nonroutine hazardous startup of the NHT heat exchangers.

The heat exchanger startup procedure specifies the use of only one outside operator to perform startup operations of the NHT heat exchanger banks. However on the day of the incident, a supervisor requested five additional operators to assist with the startup of the A/B/C heat exchanger bank.
The startup of the NHT heat exchangers was hazardous nonroutine work.

controls.name: Prevention of heat exchanger flange leaks during startup
controls.barrier_role: Prevent hydrocarbon leaks that create fire hazards during startup
controls.lod_basis: Engineering control to prevent loss of containment

supporting_text: The NHT heat exchangers frequently leaked flammable hydrocarbons during startup, sometimes resulting in fires.
Tesoro management had been complacent about these hazardous leaks and did not always investigate the cause of the leaks.
Tesoro did take some actions to prevent the leaks, but these actions did not effectively prevent the leaks before the April 2010 incident.
A history of frequent leaks and occasional fires during this startup activity.

controls.name: Improved heat exchanger design with automated valve control
controls.barrier_role: Reduce need for manual operator intervention during startup
controls.lod_basis: Engineering control to reduce operational complexity and worker exposure

supporting_text: The NHT heat exchanger banks were designed with large, difficult-to-manipulate manual block valves on different levels of the NHT heat exchanger structure.
These valves were used to start up the NHT heat exchanger banks and typically required numerous adjustments to maintain temperature specifications.
The difficulties with valve operation during startup typically resulted in the need for additional operator assistance.
The new design of the NHT heat exchangers has eliminated the need to clean the exchangers while the unit is operating.

controls.name: Process instrumentation to monitor actual operating conditions
controls.barrier_role: Provide real-time data on heat exchanger operating conditions
controls.lod_basis: Instrumentation control to enable monitoring and verification of safe operation

supporting_text: Insufficient process instrumentation.
Tesoro did not monitor actual operating conditions of the B and E heat exchangers within the NHT heat exchanger banks, even though it would have been technically feasible to do so.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
